skill.name: meituan-coupon
skill.description: Claim Meituan coupons, verify account status, and query coupon history through bundled Python scripts and Meituan API calls.
skill.summary: Meituan coupon helper for coupon claiming, account login, and redemption history lookup.
skill.author: Meituan-Open-Platform

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported extensive command, network, filesystem, environment, and obfuscation indicators. Human review confirms high-risk behavior: persistent account tokens, network requests carrying phone and token data, subprocess-based cache operations, and recurring task instructions. I did not find enough evidence to conclude confirmed malicious intent, so the skill is not marked critical or blocked.

high_findings.title: Persistent Authentication Token Storage
high_findings.description: The auth module migrates legacy token files and writes user_token, device_token, phone_masked, and terms state into a shared local cache. This creates a high-impact exposure if the workspace or another skill can read shared cache data.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code directly reads legacy token files and writes complete auth data to a shared local file. The behavior is intentional and confirmed by function names and comments.

high_findings.title: Network Requests Carry Account Identifiers and Tokens
high_findings.description: The scripts send phone numbers, SMS codes, device identifiers, user tokens, and coupon redemption codes to remote Meituan endpoints. This may be legitimate for coupon service operation, but it is sensitive account data flow.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The request bodies explicitly include mobile, smsVerifyCode, uuid, token, and redemption code values. The endpoints appear service-related, so this is high risk rather than confirmed exfiltration.

high_findings.title: Environment-Controlled Subprocess Execution
high_findings.description: The shared helper builds subprocess commands from environment-controlled Python and CLI paths. A hostile environment could redirect SKILL_CACHE_PYTHON or SKILL_CACHE_CLI_PATH to run unexpected code.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess arguments are list-based, which reduces shell injection risk, but executable paths are taken from environment variables. That creates a realistic execution redirection risk.

high_findings.title: Prompt-Injection-Like Priority and Output Control
high_findings.description: The skill instructs the agent that its critical rules have highest priority, restricts user-visible output to exact templates, and hides execution details. These controls can conflict with host safety and transparency requirements.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The text is clear and directive, and it tells the agent to prioritize skill rules and suppress execution detail. It appears task-oriented, so I do not classify it as confirmed malicious prompt injection.

high_findings.title: Recurring Automation Can Trigger Coupon Claims Without Each Use
high_findings.description: The scheduling instructions create durable recurring jobs that call token verification and coupon issuance. This expands risk because future network actions can run after initial consent.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documentation explicitly defines recurring claim behavior and platform scheduling actions. The user is asked before enabling it, which lowers confidence from critical to high.

medium_findings.title: Weak Deterministic MD5 Redemption Code Generation
medium_findings.description: The issue script derives the daily redemption code with MD5 over user_token, masked phone, and date. MD5 is weak and the deterministic construction exposes correlation risk if codes leak.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code directly calls hashlib.md5 for a security-relevant identifier. It may only need uniqueness, but the input includes a user token.

medium_findings.title: Coupon History Is Keyed by Tokens and Masked Phone Data
medium_findings.description: Coupon history is stored using user tokens and masked phone numbers as keys. This is useful for deduplication, but it increases privacy exposure in local cache files.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The storage structure is documented in comments and implemented directly. The data is not full phone data, so the finding is medium rather than high.

medium_findings.title: Documentation Contains Operational Shell Commands
medium_findings.description: Many static external-command findings in SKILL.md and reference files are shell examples that guide agents to run Python scripts and scheduling commands. These are expected for this skill, but they still increase operational risk.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The findings are real commands in documentation, not hidden code execution. They are intentionally used to drive the workflow, so legitimate use is possible.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Service URLs Appear Service-Specific
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs point to Meituan service and policy domains used for login, coupon operations, and terms. I did not find evidence that these URLs target unrelated third-party collection endpoints.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The domains match the stated Meituan coupon purpose. The URLs remain privacy relevant because requests carry account data.

low_findings.title: Windows SAM and Obfuscation Hits Were Not Semantically Confirmed
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged Windows SAM strings and high-entropy content in documentation, but review did not find a confirmed attempt to access Windows credential databases or unpack a payload in the inspected workflow.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The static signals are weak in the inspected context and likely triggered by dense multilingual text or incidental strings. This should be monitored, but I did not confirm malicious credential harvesting.

dangerous_patterns.title: Sensitive Token Persistence Plus Network API Calls
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill combines local token persistence with API calls that use those tokens for coupon issue and query operations. This combination requires strict workspace isolation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The persistence and network use are both direct and central to the skill design. The risk depends on source trust and local cache isolation.

dangerous_patterns.title: Environment-Influenced Process Launch
dangerous_patterns.description: The cache helper can launch a Python executable and CLI path selected through environment variables. This is a dangerous pattern when a skill runs in an untrusted or shared environment.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command is constructed without a shell, but the executable path and CLI path are environment-influenced. That supports a clear but conditional risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: Durable Scheduled Actions
dangerous_patterns.description: The reference workflow asks the agent to create recurring coupon claim tasks on supported platforms. Durable automation changes future account behavior after setup.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The instructions explicitly create recurring tasks. User confirmation is described, so this is a dangerous pattern rather than confirmed abuse.

content.user_title: Claim Meituan Coupons and Track Benefits
content.value_statement: Users need a guided way to claim Meituan coupons and check prior coupon results. This skill automates login checks, coupon issuance, and history lookup with local scripts.
actual_capabilities: Checks local Meituan authentication state before coupon workflows.
Sends SMS login requests and verifies user-provided codes.
Claims daily coupon packages through the Meituan coupon issue endpoint.
Queries historical coupon redemption records by date or date range.
Stores coupon history and masked phone history in local skill cache files.
Provides instructions for optional recurring coupon claiming schedules.
limitations: Requires a valid Meituan account and SMS verification for first use.
Depends on Meituan API availability and configured channel values.
Stores authentication and coupon history locally, which needs careful access control.
Automatic scheduling depends on platform support outside the skill.

use_cases.title: Claim daily coupons
use_cases.description: Start the coupon flow, verify login status, and request today's Meituan coupon package.
use_cases.target_user: Meituan users who regularly claim discounts

use_cases.title: Review coupon history
use_cases.description: Check previously claimed coupons for a single day or a date range.
use_cases.target_user: Users tracking coupon usage and validity

use_cases.title: Configure recurring reminders
use_cases.description: Save a preferred daily claim time and receive platform-specific scheduling instructions.
use_cases.target_user: Users who want recurring coupon workflows

prompt_templates.title: Claim today's coupons
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me claim today's Meituan coupons and tell me what was issued.

prompt_templates.title: Check login before claiming
prompt_templates.prompt: Check whether my Meituan login is valid, then guide me through claiming coupons if needed.

prompt_templates.title: Review recent coupon records
prompt_templates.prompt: Show my Meituan coupon records for the last three days and summarize active coupons.

prompt_templates.title: Set a daily claim schedule
prompt_templates.prompt: Set up a daily Meituan coupon claim workflow at 10:00 Beijing time and explain any platform setup needed.

output_examples.output: The assistant checks login status, requests the daily coupon package, and reports coupon names, amounts, and validity dates.

output_examples.input: What coupons did I claim this week?
output_examples.output: The assistant searches stored redemption records and summarizes matching coupon history by date.

output_examples.input: Remind me to claim coupons every morning.
output_examples.output: The assistant saves the preferred time and provides the scheduling action supported by the current platform.

best_practices: Use the skill only after reviewing how it stores tokens and coupon history locally.
Verify the Meituan API endpoint and source repository before entering a phone number or SMS code.
Keep the workspace private because cached authentication data can persist across sessions.
anti_patterns: Do not install this skill in a shared workspace without isolating its cache directory.
Do not allow untrusted agents or skills to override script paths or environment variables.
Do not enable recurring coupon claims unless the scheduling platform is trusted.

faq.question: Does this skill require a Meituan account?
faq.answer: Yes. It uses SMS login and token verification before claiming coupons.

faq.question: Does it make network requests?
faq.answer: Yes. It posts login, token verification, coupon issue, and query requests to Meituan endpoints.

faq.question: Where is authentication data stored?
faq.answer: The scripts store user tokens, device tokens, and related state in local skill cache files.

faq.question: Can it claim coupons automatically every day?
faq.answer: It includes scheduling instructions, but actual scheduling depends on the host platform.

faq.question: Is this safe for shared machines?
faq.answer: It is not recommended for shared machines because it persists account and coupon data locally.

faq.question: Why is this marketplace listing marked high risk?
faq.answer: The audit confirmed sensitive token storage, network calls with account data, subprocess execution, and recurring automation instructions.
